risk_description: UFLPA enforcement on electronics from China is intensifying. CBP detention volumes are rising and the UFLPA Entity List is expanding to new Chinese manufacturers of electronics. Milwaukee Tool's Dongguan operations face increasing supply chain tracing burdens to demonstrate UFLPA compliance.
primary_restriction_instrument: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA) — CBP Enforcement Escalation
secondary_restriction_instrument: US CBP Enforcement Data; UFLPA Entity List; Congressional-Executive Commission on China (CECC)
context: US CBP quarterly detention data shows a sustained year-on-year increase in UFLPA-related holds on Chinese-origin goods. The UFLPA Entity List receives quarterly updates adding new Chinese manufacturers. Congressional oversight hearings in 2025 have called for expanded enforcement beyond XUAR-linked goods to any goods where forced labour cannot be excluded from the supply chain.
monitoring_signals: Monitor UFLPA Entity List quarterly updates (CBP.gov); US CBP trade statistics for China-origin holds; Congressional CECC reports; Kharon and Sayari Analytics entity screening for supplier relationships

risk_description: UFLPA rebuttable presumption applies to cotton lint with any nexus to XUAR. ZURU's toy manufacturing in Guangdong relies on textile and cotton inputs that may contain XUAR-origin fibre. US importers of Chinese toys face increasing CBP scrutiny and must maintain granular supply chain tracing documentation.
primary_restriction_instrument: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA) — Textile/Cotton Enforcement
secondary_restriction_instrument: US CBP; Cotton Campaign; UFLPA Entity List; Kharon Analytics
context: XUAR cotton and textiles remain subject to rebuttable presumption under UFLPA. Enforcement is not declining; CBP has expanded the universe of downstream products subject to scrutiny to include mixed-content goods where XUAR-origin fibre cannot be ruled out through supply chain tracing. Toy and apparel supply chains are a priority enforcement focus.
monitoring_signals: Monitor UFLPA XUAR-specific enforcement releases; Cotton Campaign reporting; Oritain and Haelixa fibre-tracing technology for supply chain verification

risk_level: Likely within 12 months — compliance programmes must begin now
risk_description: EU CSDDD transposition requires EU-bound supply chains to conduct human rights due diligence on electronics sourced from China. Companies must identify, prevent, and mitigate adverse impacts. Non-compliance by 2027 will carry civil liability and injunctive remedies under national law.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) 2024/1760
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal (2024/1760); European Commission CSDDD Guidance
context: EU CSDDD (Directive 2024/1760) entered into force July 2024. EU member states have until July 2026 to transpose into national law. The largest companies (>5,000 employees / €1.5B turnover) face compliance obligations from 2027. Forward-looking due diligence programmes must begin immediately. Procurement teams are now required to assess and address adverse human rights impacts in supply chains from high-risk sourcing regions.
monitoring_signals: Monitor national transposition progress via EUR-Lex; EU Commission guidance documents; industry association compliance guidance (amfori, ecovadis); supplier self-assessment questionnaire rollout timelines

risk_description: EUDR enforcement requires GOJO to demonstrate that cattle sourced from Brazil for soap and surfactant production does not originate from deforested or degraded land after December 2020. Additionally, ILAB and NGO monitoring of Brazilian agricultural labour conditions intensifies CSDDD-linked due diligence requirements for EU-destined goods.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) 2023/1115
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; ILAB Brazil Country Report; Rainforest Alliance; Global Forest Watch
context: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) enforcement for large operators began December 2024 (delayed from original date). The regulation creates mandatory due diligence and traceability requirements for cattle, cocoa, coffee, palm oil, soya, timber, rubber, and derived products. Brazil is the highest-risk source country for cattle and cocoa. Failure to demonstrate deforestation-free sourcing will block EU market access.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Commission EUDR implementation portal; Brazil deforestation monitoring (PRODES/DETER, MapBiomas); EUDR traceability platform development; EUDR enforcement announcements from EU member state competent authorities

risk_description: EUDR enforcement requires GOJO to demonstrate that cocoa sourced from Brazil for soap and surfactant production does not originate from deforested or degraded land after December 2020. Additionally, ILAB and NGO monitoring of Brazilian agricultural labour conditions intensifies CSDDD-linked due diligence requirements for EU-destined goods.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) 2023/1115
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; ILAB Brazil Country Report; Rainforest Alliance; Global Forest Watch
context: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) enforcement for large operators began December 2024 (delayed from original date). The regulation creates mandatory due diligence and traceability requirements for cattle, cocoa, coffee, palm oil, soya, timber, rubber, and derived products. Brazil is the highest-risk source country for cattle and cocoa. Failure to demonstrate deforestation-free sourcing will block EU market access.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Commission EUDR implementation portal; Brazil deforestation monitoring (PRODES/DETER, MapBiomas); EUDR traceability platform development; EUDR enforcement announcements from EU member state competent authorities

risk_level: Compliance obligation within 12 months — direct restriction is tail risk
risk_description: Sugarcane is not an EUDR commodity. The restriction pathway is through EU CSDDD mandatory due diligence: companies sourcing Brazilian sugarcane for surfactant production must assess and remediate any forced labour or child labour in the supply chain. Non-compliance carries civil liability under CSDDD. Direct goods restriction would require a formal EU Forced Labour Regulation investigation finding — currently a tail-risk scenario for Brazilian sugarcane specifically.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU CSDDD 2024/1760 — Supply Chain Due Diligence Obligation
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; ILAB Brazil Country Report; Rainforest Alliance; Global Forest Watch
context: Brazil sugarcane sector has documented child labour exposure (ILAB-listed). EU CSDDD will require due diligence on sugarcane supply chains. However, sugarcane is not an EUDR commodity; there is no automatic market access denial mechanism. Compliance obligation is real; outright restriction is contingent on a formal EU Forced Labour Regulation investigation.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Commission EUDR implementation portal; Brazil deforestation monitoring (PRODES/DETER, MapBiomas); EUDR traceability platform development; EUDR enforcement announcements from EU member state competent authorities

risk_level: Compliance obligation within 12 months — direct restriction is tail risk only
risk_description: Fish is not an EUDR commodity. However, EU CSDDD creates a mandatory due diligence obligation for Brazilian fish sourced for soap and surfactant formulations. ILAB documents forced labour in Brazilian fishing sector. If due diligence reveals forced labour practices, companies face civil liability under CSDDD and must remediate or terminate the sourcing relationship. Direct import restriction would require an EU Forced Labour Regulation formal investigation, which is a tail-risk pathway.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU CSDDD 2024/1760 — Supply Chain Due Diligence Obligation
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; ILAB Brazil Country Report; Rainforest Alliance; Global Forest Watch
context: Brazil fish sector (including sardine, tuna, and anchovy processing for industrial fish oil) is monitored by ILAB for forced labour. EU CSDDD transposition in 2025-2026 will require due diligence on these supply chains. Note: fish is not subject to EUDR; the restriction pathway is CSDDD compliance obligation and potential EU Forced Labour Regulation investigation, not automatic market access denial.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Commission EUDR implementation portal; Brazil deforestation monitoring (PRODES/DETER, MapBiomas); EUDR traceability platform development; EUDR enforcement announcements from EU member state competent authorities

risk_level: Likely within 12 months — annual ILAB review cycle
risk_description: ILAB annual monitoring of Brazilian cattle sector labour conditions continues. Brazil remains on US government watch lists for forced labour in cattle ranching and charcoal production. Annual ILAB TVPRA list update (expected late 2025) may update risk rating.

risk_level: Maintained within 12 months — USMCA review ongoing
risk_description: Mexican cattle production remains subject to ILAB monitoring. USMCA labour provisions create ongoing compliance obligations for US importers. Risk is maintained at current level pending 2025 USMCA review outcome.
primary_restriction_instrument: USMCA Chapter 23 Labour Provisions — Rapid Response Mechanism
secondary_restriction_instrument: ILAB 2024 TVPRA List; USTR USMCA Rapid Response Reports
context: USMCA Rapid Response Mechanism has been invoked multiple times since 2021 against Mexican manufacturing facilities. Labour standards enforcement under Chapter 23 is ongoing. The 2025 USMCA joint review may tighten enforcement provisions further. Mexican manufacturing labour compliance is an active monitoring priority for US importers.
monitoring_signals: Monitor USTR USMCA Rapid Response Mechanism reports; Mexican Ministry of Labour (STPS) collective bargaining agreement registry; ILO Better Work Mexico programme outputs

risk_level: Possible within 12 months — annual review expected
risk_description: Thai fish sector forced labour risk affects fish oil and marine ingredient supply chains. GOJO's soap and surfactant formulations using marine-derived fatty acids or fish oil may carry exposure to Thai fishing sector forced labour practices. ILAB monitoring and annual TIP Report assessment confirm ongoing risk.
primary_restriction_instrument: ILAB TVPRA List — Thailand Fish/Shrimp; US Trafficking Victims Protection Act
secondary_restriction_instrument: US State Dept TIP Report 2024; ILO Thailand Fishing Sector Reports; ILAB Country Reports
context: Thailand's seafood sector remains subject to ongoing ILO, US State Department, and EU monitoring for forced labour, particularly in the deep-sea fishing and shrimp processing sectors. Thailand was on the US State Dept Tier 2 Watchlist in prior years and continues to require active monitoring. Fish oil sourced from Thai suppliers for soap and surfactant formulations carries compliance risk.
monitoring_signals: Monitor US State Dept TIP Report annual Thailand assessment; ILO Better Work Thailand; EU IUU fishing enforcement reports; Thai DFT (Department of Fisheries) catch documentation scheme; Seafood Task Force Thailand progress

risk_level: Compliance due diligence obligation within 12 months — restriction is tail risk
risk_description: EU CSDDD creates a mandatory due diligence obligation for supply chains involving South African coal (via steel inputs). CBAM is a carbon pricing instrument and does not restrict goods on labour grounds. The pathway to actual goods restriction requires: (a) CSDDD due diligence revealing forced or exploitative labour in South African coal mining; (b) failure to remediate; and (c) a formal EU Forced Labour Regulation investigation and market withdrawal order. This is a multi-step tail-risk pathway, not a near-term restriction signal. Flagged as a compliance monitoring obligation.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU CSDDD 2024/1760 — Supply Chain Labour Due Diligence
secondary_restriction_instrument: South African Human Rights Commission; ILO South Africa Mining Reports; EU CSDDD Guidance
context: South African coal mining labour conditions are documented by SAHRC and NUM/AMCU union reports as including wage suppression and unsafe conditions in some operations. EU CSDDD requires due diligence but does not create a direct import restriction. CBAM applies to embedded carbon content, not labour practices — citing CBAM as a labour restriction instrument would be incorrect. A realistic restriction pathway requires CSDDD non-compliance findings escalated to an EU Forced Labour Regulation investigation. Confidence in actual restriction within 12 months is low.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU CBAM registry for South African coal importers; South African Human Rights Commission mining sector reports; NUM/AMCU union reports; EU CSDDD transposition progress affecting South African supplier due diligence

risk_level: Possible within 12 months — CBP enforcement expanding
risk_description: Garmin Taiwan-assembled electronics containing PRC-sourced components face UFLPA scrutiny at US ports of entry. CBP has issued guidance that UFLPA rebuttable presumption can apply to Taiwan-assembled goods with upstream Chinese-origin inputs. Supply chain tracing burden is increasing.
primary_restriction_instrument: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA) — Component Tracing Expansion
secondary_restriction_instrument: US CBP UFLPA Enforcement Guidance; UFLPA Entity List; Garmin SEC 10-K Filings
context: UFLPA enforcement is extending scrutiny to electronics assembled in Taiwan but containing components or materials sourced from Mainland China, particularly XUAR-region inputs. Garmin's Taiwan plants (Lin Kou, Zhongli, Tainan) assemble precision electronics using component supply chains that may include PRC-origin parts. CBP has issued guidance noting that UFLPA applies to goods regardless of final assembly location if PRC-origin inputs are present.
monitoring_signals: Monitor CBP UFLPA enforcement guidance updates; Garmin SEC filing supply chain disclosures; UFLPA Entity List for Taiwanese-based companies with PRC operations; US-Taiwan supply chain tracing initiatives

risk_level: Enforcement in force — risk materialising now for EU-destined goods
risk_description: EUDR enforcement requires that cattle sourced from Argentina for soap and surfactant production is demonstrably free from Gran Chaco deforestation linkage (post Dec 2020). Argentine cattle supply chains lack the satellite-monitored traceability infrastructure of Brazilian counterparts, making EUDR compliance documentation difficult to obtain. Failure to provide compliant due diligence statements will deny EU market access for affected goods. This is a direct market restriction mechanism, not merely a compliance burden.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) 2023/1115 — Argentine Cattle / Gran Chaco
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; Global Forest Watch Chaco data; Trase.earth supply chain mapping; WWF Gran Chaco assessment
context: Argentina's Gran Chaco is one of the world's most actively deforested biomes, losing over 200,000 hectares annually. Argentine beef cattle ranching is the primary driver of Gran Chaco deforestation. The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) applies to cattle and beef and requires that goods do not originate from land deforested after December 2020. Argentina has no national deforestation monitoring system equivalent to Brazil's PRODES/DETER for the Chaco, creating significant traceability challenges. EUDR enforcement for large operators began December 2024. Argentine beef without verified Chaco-deforestation-free supply chain tracing faces genuine EU market access denial risk.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Commission EUDR due diligence portal; Trase.earth Argentina cattle deforestation risk scores; Argentine deforestation monitoring (REDAF Chaco data); EUDR competent authority enforcement actions against Argentine beef importers
